examples.title: Linear Search using Recursion
examples.description: A basic recursive implementation of linear search that finds the index of a target element in an unsorted array
examples.code:
def linear_search_recursive(arr, target, index=0):
    """
    Recursively search for target in array
    
    Args:
        arr: List of elements to search
        target: Element to find
        index: Current position (starts at 0)
    
    Returns:
        Index of target if found, -1 otherwise
    """
    # Base case: element not found
    if index >= len(arr):
        return -1
    
    # Base case: element found
    if arr[index] == target:
        return index
    
    # Recursive case: check next element
    return linear_search_recursive(arr, target, index + 1)


# Test the function
data = [23, 45, 12, 67, 89, 34, 56]
target = 67

result = linear_search_recursive(data, target)
if result != -1:
    print(f"Element {target} found at index {result}")
else:
    print(f"Element {target} not found")
examples.explanation:
1. Function starts at index 0
2. Base case 1: If index equals array length, element not found, return -1
3. Base case 2: If current element matches target, return its index
4. Recursive case: Call function with next index (index + 1)
5. Each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack

examples.title: Binary Search using Recursion
examples.description: Classic recursive binary search implementation for sorted arrays with efficient O(log n) time complexity
examples.code:
def binary_search_recursive(arr, target, low, high):
    """
    Recursively search for target in sorted array using divide and conquer
    
    Args:
        arr: Sorted list of elements (must be sorted)
        target: Element to find
        low: Starting index of search range
        high: Ending index of search range
    
    Returns:
        Index of target if found, -1 otherwise
    """
    # Base case: invalid range (element not found)
    if low > high:
        return -1
    
    # Calculate middle index to avoid overflow
    mid = low + (high - low) // 2
    
    # Base case: element found at mid
    if arr[mid] == target:
        return mid
    
    # Recursive case: search left half
    elif arr[mid] > target:
        return binary_search_recursive(arr, target, low, mid - 1)
    
    # Recursive case: search right half
    else:
        return binary_search_recursive(arr, target, mid + 1, high)


# Test the function
sorted_data = [11, 22, 33, 44, 55, 66, 77, 88, 99]
target = 55

result = binary_search_recursive(sorted_data, target, 0, len(sorted_data) - 1)
if result != -1:
    print(f"Element {target} found at index {result}")
else:
    print(f"Element {target} not found")
examples.explanation:
1. Requires sorted array (prerequisite for binary search)
2. Calculate mid point of current search range
3. Base case: If mid element equals target, return index
4. Recursive case 1: If target < mid, search left half (low to mid-1)
5. Recursive case 2: If target > mid, search right half (mid+1 to high)
6. Base case: If low > high, element not found
7. Each recursion halves the search space

examples.title: Find All Occurrences using Binary Search Recursion
examples.description: Advanced recursive implementation that finds ALL indices where target appears in a sorted array (handles duplicates)
examples.explanation:
1. Uses two helper functions: one finds leftmost, one finds rightmost
2. Leftmost search: When target found at mid, check if previous element is also target; if not, this is leftmost
3. Rightmost search: When target found at mid, check if next element is also target; if not, this is rightmost
4. Combine results to get all occurrences
5. Demonstrates practical handling of duplicate elements in sorted data
6. Useful for real-world scenarios like finding all occurrences of a word in a document
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n + k) where k is number of occurrences
